Off Calendar Sep-09-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. EDWARD M. LAI, Esq. of Fried & Williams LLP (Tel: 415-421-0100) for Plaintiff Katherine Roberts;
Atty. MARISSA N. ACREE, Esq. of Stone & Associates (Tel: 925-938-1555) for Defendants Swanrock, Inc. dba Skytech Solar, Colin Swan, and Joel Frangquist.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT JOEL FRANGQUIST, SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories And Production Of Documents.

Both sides stipulate to the mattter being decided by the judge pro tem. Counsel and the judge pro tem all appearing remotely authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with additions, as follows: Defendants' Motion to Compel and Request for Sanctions is GRANTED; Defendants' request for an order precluding Plaintiff from offering the withheld evidence is DENIED without prejudice. The Motion to Compel identifies discoverable evidence which has not been produced over a long period, including through many months of attempts to informally resolve discovery disputes. An offer to ultimately produce additional documents without a date certain does not preclude motion practice, particularly when an agreed motion deadline approaches. There is no evidentiary support for Plaintiff's claim that COVID-19 or a health condition prevented her from responding to discovery, much less from providing a deadline for the completion of the admittedly inadequate discovery responses. The request for a preclusion order regarding non-produced evidence was not supported by any legal authority nor substantive legal argument and is therefore DENIED without prejudice. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is DENIED. Plaintiff is to make a first discovery response by 9/23/20 of documents plaintiff is able to obtain through signed releases and with verifications where possible, and with a description for documents that are still part of an investigation that require client participation. Plaintiff is to make a final discovery response by 11/2/20 of all responses and verifications.

Defense counsel is to prepare and submit a proposed order to the judge pro tem at rebecca@greyresolution.com.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Consolidate Case No. Cgc-18-569262 With Case No. Cgc-17-562223; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declaration Of Marissa N. Acree In Support Thereof; And Proposed Order
Off Calendar Apr-04-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Edward M. Lai, Esq., of Fred & Wiliam attorney at law, 415-421-0100, appearing for Plaintiff;
Marisa N. Acree, Ssq, Stone & Associates, 925-938-1555, appearing for Defendant Swanrock {CourtCall)

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR's Motion To Consolidate Case No. CGC-18-569262 With Case No. CGC-17-562223

GRANTED and case No. CGC-17-562223 shall be the lead case. Because the two actions pending before this court involve common questions of law and fact, they are consolidated for all purposes pursuant to Code Civ. Proc., 1048. Both actions concern defendant Swanrock's installation of solar panels on the roof of plaintiff Katherine Roberts' building and the damage Ms. Roberts suffered as a result of the allegedly wrongful conduct of Swanrock related to this installation. The actions should be litigated as one to avoid unnecessary costs, inefficiencies, and the possibility of inconsistent rulings.

Because of the subrogation doctrine, the insurer State Farm, which has partially compensated Ms. Roberts for her loss, and Ms. Roberts both have the right to seek recovery of damages from Swanrock. (Allstate Ins. Co. v. Mel Rapton, Inc. (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 901, 908.) When as, here, State Farm has not sought to intervene in its insured's action but chose instead to file a separate action, Swanrock can properly move to consolidate the two actions. (See Hodge v. Kirkpatrick Development, Inc. (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 540, 550.)

Ms. Roberts' reliance on Evidence Code 1155, which governs the admissibility of evidence at trial, is misplaced, and she cites no authority to support her argument that consolidation under such circumstances should not be granted because of possible prejudice to the plaintiff. Ms. Roberts' concern appears to be related to the application of the collateral source rule. However, the collateral source rule does not prevent an insurer from recovering from the third party tortfeasor in subrogation. (See Garbell v. Conejo Hardwoods, Inc. (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 1563, 1572.) Any concerns regarding the admissibility of evidence at trial concerning State Farm's identity or partial payments to Ms. Roberts may be addressed in pretrial motions in limine. Prevailing party to submit form order that complies with CRC 3.1312. and email the proposed order to contestedept30tr@sftc.org.

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT COLIN SWAN, JOEL FRANGQUIST, SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR's DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT FOR DAMAGES.

Defendants Swanrock, Inc. dba Skytech Solar, Colin Swan, and Joel Frangquist's demurrer to the intentional misrepresentation cause of action is SUSTAINED with 20 days leave to amend. Roberts has leave to allege specific facts as to each defendant to support the intentional misrepresentation claim and/or plead a concealment claim per CACI 1901. It appears that the gravamen of the claim is that defendants actively concealed Learned's unlicensed status to solicit Roberts and that caused her damage. The demurrer to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. "Whether a fiduciary duty exists is generally a question of law." (Marzec v. Public Employees' Retirement System (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 889, 915.) Here, the contractor-hirer relationship is not a recognized fiduciary relationship such as guardian-ward, trustee-beneficiary, etc. (See Richelle L. v. Roman Catholic Archbishop (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 257, 271.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Off Calendar Sep-11-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance by any party or counsel.

DEFENDANTS SWANROCK, INC. DBA SKYTECH SOLAR, COLIN SWAN, AND JOEL FRANGQUIST'S DEMURRER to FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

There being no appearances, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Swanrock Inc., Colin Swan, and Joel Frangquist's demurrer to the First Amended Complaint is SUSTAINED with 20 days leave to amend as to the Sixth and Ninth Causes of Action. "A plaintiff alleging unfair business practices under these statutes must state with reasonable particularity the facts supporting the statutory elements of the violation." (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 619.) Plaintiff Roberts fails to highlight the precise statutes violated in the body of the Sixth Cause of Action. She also fails to expressly pray for restitution and/or an injunction. The prior references to statutory violations at paragraphs 44-45 do not make it clear that they are the basis for the unlawful business practice cause of action. Paragraph 64 should be deleted as well because plaintiff admits that she is only suing on her own behalf and not on behalf of others. If she were suing on behalf of others, she would need to allege facts establishing her compliance with Code Civ. Proc. 382.

Plaintiff also has 20 days leave to amend the Ninth Cause of Action for Intentional Misrepresentation. "The specificity requirement means a plaintiff must allege facts showing how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were made, and, in the case of a corporate defendant, the plaintiff must allege the names of the persons who made the representations, their authority to speak on behalf of the corporation, to whom they spoke, what they said or wrote, and when the representation was made." (West v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 780, 793.) Order signed. (see order for full details)
